name: Service Health Check Script with Notifications
description: Build a bash script that monitors service endpoints, checks response codes and latency, and sends alerts when services are down.

steps.title: Check a single endpoint
steps.description: Use curl to verify a service returns HTTP 200 and measure response time.
steps.tip: -s silences progress, -o /dev/null discards body, -w prints status code and timing.

steps.title: Create a multi-service health check script
steps.description: Loop through a list of endpoints and check each one.
steps.command:
cat > healthcheck.sh << 'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

ENDPOINTS=(
  "http://localhost:3000/health"
  "http://localhost:3001/api/health"
  "https://api.example.com/status"
)

for url in "${ENDPOINTS[@]}"; do
  HTTP_CODE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 "$url" || echo "000")
  if [[ "$HTTP_CODE" -eq 200 ]]; then
    echo "[OK]   $url ($HTTP_CODE)"
  else
    echo "[FAIL] $url ($HTTP_CODE)" >&2
  fi
done
EOF
chmod +x healthcheck.sh

steps.title: Add response time monitoring
steps.description: Track response times and flag slow endpoints above a threshold.
steps.command: echo 'RESPONSE_TIME=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{time_total}" --max-time 10 "$url")' && echo 'if (( $(echo "$RESPONSE_TIME > 2.0" | bc -l) )); then echo "[SLOW] $url (${RESPONSE_TIME}s)"; fi'

steps.title: Add webhook notification on failure
steps.description: Send an alert to Slack or Discord when a health check fails.
steps.warning: Store webhook URLs in environment variables, not in the script. Never commit secrets to version control.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Service Health Checker
ENDPOINTS=(
  "http://localhost:3000/health"
  "http://localhost:3001/api/health"
)

TIMEOUT=5
SLOW_THRESHOLD=2.0
WEBHOOK_URL="${SLACK_WEBHOOK:-}"
FAILURES=0

log() { echo "[$(date +'%H:%M:%S')] $*"; }

for url in "${ENDPOINTS[@]}"; do
  RESPONSE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} %{time_total}' --max-time "$TIMEOUT" "$url" 2>/dev/null || echo "000 0")
  HTTP_CODE=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | awk '{print $1}')
  DURATION=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | awk '{print $2}')

  if [[ "$HTTP_CODE" -eq 200 ]]; then
    log "[OK]   $url (${DURATION}s)"
  else
    log "[FAIL] $url (HTTP $HTTP_CODE)" >&2
    ((FAILURES++))
    if [[ -n "$WEBHOOK_URL" ]]; then
      curl -s -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
        -d "{"text": "ALERT: $url returned HTTP $HTTP_CODE"}" \
        "$WEBHOOK_URL" > /dev/null
    fi
  fi
done

if [[ $FAILURES -gt 0 ]]; then
  log "$FAILURES service(s) failing"
  exit 1
fi
log "All services healthy"

faq.question: How often should I run health checks?
faq.answer: Every 30-60 seconds for critical services, every 5 minutes for non-critical. Use cron for minute-level checks: '* * * * * /path/to/healthcheck.sh'. For sub-minute intervals, use a loop with sleep inside the script.

faq.question: What should a health endpoint return?
faq.answer: Return HTTP 200 with a JSON body like {"status":"ok","db":"connected","redis":"connected"}. Check all critical dependencies (database, cache, external APIs) and return 503 if any are unhealthy.

faq.question: Should I use bash or a proper monitoring tool?
faq.answer: Bash health checks are great for simple setups and quick monitoring. For production, consider tools like Prometheus + Grafana, Datadog, or UptimeRobot that provide dashboards, alerting, and historical data.
